sentences.text: Yeah.
The last day of Dachau, we saw that the Germans are losing the war because where I was walking in that place, the American Air Force, the American airplanes, were bombing Germany all over.
And when their airplanes used to come, they used to have the sirens blowing, the Germans, to hide.
Take off, you know, from your places and hide himself in that place, in the bunkers there.
Where the building for to make that secret weapon.
All the Germans run wild to save their lives.
Even the guards used to run away, leave us alone, the prisoners.
For them to hide, because they never knew where the bombs are going to fall.
So we were without guards, but we couldn't run nowhere.
We were so deep in Germany that you cannot run, and you are skeletons.
You cannot do anything.
But any time I looked up while the Germans were hiding, and I saw the airplanes, the Americans going heavy loaded with bombs.
And I looked it up in the skies.
I said, God bless you.
I said, oh, come safe home.
And the airplanes used to let out, you know, the silver paper so they couldn't be spotted, you know, to shut them down, you know.
Like to say-- I forgot what it's called, you know, in the Air Force.
Those things.
You know, like sliced silver, you know, shavings.
So the-- the guns cannot know if it's the airplanes to distract them, you know.
But I saw the airplanes.
Some of them very little because they were flying very high.
And they were bombing somewheres, in other places.
But the Germans, the guards, didn't know where.
So they used to hide themselves.
And I was so happy.
Yeah, it signaled more the end of the war.
When we saw bomb-- the airplanes all the time, we had to run.
The Germans had to run, and we were left without guards in the working place, we knew.
And the Germans were so nasty that times.
They knew they are losing the war.
We were counting the days.
We didn't know what's going to happen to us.
We still thought that if they lose-- if-- before the Americans are going to liberate us or whoever is going to liberate us, the Germans are going to shoot us.
And that's what the plan was.
The plan was all the prisoners from all the camps to be shot before the liberation.
But thank God it didn't work out that way.
Instead, the main chief who had the order to liquidate all the prisoners from Dachau didn't give out the order.
He want to save his own life.
He knew the war is lost.
And he figured if the Americans come in and he'll show that he had the order to shoot all the prisoners and he didn't release it, he's not going to be hung.
He's not going to be punished.
So that's how we were saved.
I think his name was Martin Weiss.
The prisoners, after the-- we were all chased for the dead march from Dachau all the way to the Tyrol mountains in big caves, natural caves.
All the prisoners from all the camps all around, not only Dachau, were placed in that valley, a natural valley near the Tyrol mountains.
Maybe three, four miles valley.
Deep.
You go deep and deep and deep.
And all the machine guns were placed all on the top, ready to fire and shoot at people.
And that was the May-- May the 30th.
We were all chased-- May the 30th, we were chased day and night all the way from Dachau to the Tyrol mountains.
When we arrived to that valley, was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] May the-- May the 31st.
May.
Yeah.
May has got a 31.
31 day.
Was May the 31st, we arrived.
And it was very dark in the nighttime.
And the we stumbled-- stubbled one on top of the other, it was so dark.
Thousands of prisoners were already there from other camps.
They were already there from-- brought before us.
And I stumbled on a prisoner, walked on him, because it's too dark.
And he was a German prisoner.
Must have been a [GERMAN]-- [GERMAN] German prisoner.
And he spoke in German to me.
And he said to me, where do you coming from?
I says, from Dachau.
He says, yeah.
I came from Dachau too, but from a prior transport couple of days before.
He says-- tonight, he says, is our last night.
Did you see the machine guns all around?
I says, no.
It's dark.
We came, it was dark in the nighttime.
He says, in the morning, I think, they'll start shooting.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
May the 1st in the morning it was snowing.
Because it's near Tyrol, the mountains.
People are snowing there even sometime in June, they have snow.
Tyrol, they-- it was snowing.
We were freezing to death.
People were dying there in the valley one after the other.
And in the morning when we got up, it's like little mountains with snow was lifting up, because we were all sitting all crunched in like that.
And then when it-- morning came, and we start to strain us up, it's like little snow mountains, thousands of them were lifting themselves.
And we washed off the snow and we were all shaking, shivering.
We were cold, wet.
Thin clothes.
You know, concentration camp is like clothes, like it's-- it's a shirt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Smoke was going from the human bodies, you know, from the water on the-- on the skin, what you still have left on your bodies.
And we were looking around there.
Yeah.
They were all on the top, SS troops ready to shoot.
Ready to get the order to machine gun.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a doctor not far from me, from Lithuania too, Dr.
Blumberg, and his son.
And he was a German Jew, spoke fluently German.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He came to one of the guards.
And he had in his pocket the cross, you know, like a doctors wear.
He took out from his pocket, put it on on his arm.
And he came to a guard, lucky.
And he says to the German guard, can I take out five prison-- five of the prisoners?
They are about to die if they are not getting a little warmed, a little heat.
Please let me take them out.
And right near that valley was a-- was a small little farm village.
It's called Greiling.
Greiling near Bad Toelz.
The place was Bad Toelz.
That was a place, Bad Toelz, where the German high officers used to come for vacation.
The doctor took five people.
But then I saw him taken, and he-- I knew him.
I says, Dr.
Blumberg.
So he was afraid because of me.
I'm going to spoil the thing.
So he showed me on the left side was a gutter, like water is going through, you know, from the little village into that valley.
Almost a six foot, you know, like-- like--
I know.
Like a pipe.
Pipes.
Pipes.
You know, but made out of metal thin.
And it was open, and here was going the water from that little village, a farm village, from Greiling, the water.
So Blumberg said to me I should go in in these pipes there.
And it was like from him like maybe 60 or 80 feet away, but he knew.
So I bent myself down.
I laid myself down, and I crawled to the pipes.
And these pipes maybe about 200 feet from that little farm house what was going.
I crawled through there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And it led me right to the-- a little farm house.
I knocked on the door, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and a German girl opened the door.
Go away.
We'll be shot.
The Germans are-- SS is all around.
I says, please help me.
Let me in.
And I cried.
And she saw too that the German-- the Americans are only 10 miles from Bad Toelz.
And she was a very religious person, and maybe she had a mind that she was hiding a prisoner, she was not going to be shot.
Because a lot of these German people and the farm people thought the American Army are going to shoot them with machine guns like the Germans did.
They didn't know what democracy was.
So they thought they are going to all be shot.
So she figured-- I don't know what in her mind was.
I'm not a [INAUDIBLE] and I'm not a god.
But she says, come in schnell.
Come in fast.
She opened the door and I came in very fast in that stable, where the cows-- eight or 10 cows-- were all standing right there, and hid in the hay.
And when I walked in in that stable, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] paradise.
Heaven.
The warmth from the cows, from the hay, from that stable, from being frozen so many days, being chased through woods, cities.
And from that valley all night in the-- in the snow and in the cold.
When you walk in in a stable from cows-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people got to have experienced that, to be frozen and walking in the stable of cows.
It's like adrenaline to your heart.
The warmth took me around so much that I felt I'm going to fall asleep right now.
And she was hiding me where the cows, you know, eat the hay, you know, from the wooden, you know-- I don't know in English how to--
--how to call that, this--
Trough.
Thank you.
The cows were eating from the troughs.
She said I should go under the troughs.
And she covered me with hay.
And when I was covered with the hay under the troughs in the stable, really warmed.
I said, maybe there is a God still.
But what is it my brother, I was thinking.
What is with Abramkin?
My brother didn't get a chance to make it.
It happened, everything, so fast, the situation that he remained still in the valley there with all the hundred thousands, maybe, prisoners.
How long did you remain--
So I was right there.
Hungry.
And the warmth took me around, and the girl brought out a big piece of farm bread, with a glass of hot milk.
And I was more in heaven.
And when I drank the milk and the bread, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm ashamed to tell you.
I fell asleep right under the cows.
I didn't know what happened to me.
I fell asleep.
And I was right in the stable.
Seven-- by 5 o'clock in the morning, the girl came in in the stable, and she bumped on my shoulder.
And she say to me, Juder.
Jew.
The Americans are three miles from my farm house.
I could hear already like heavy duty, you know, machinery.
Already rolling the street.
And while I'm right here and the happiness is on my face, maybe I-- I am safe.
All of a sudden, a thing happened that I-- it's hard to explain.
I hear somebody knocking in the door, but with a big healthy force fist, knocking on the door with big, you know, bangs, and speaking in German.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
Open the door.
And I hear somebody walked in.
And the girl says to that man who walked in-- it's a barn, you know, farm.
You have place there on the top go up, on the steps.
And who do you think walked up on the steps?
SS men.
A guard with his rifle and his aluminum dish, you know?
I forgot-- the canteen what they eat.
I hear him walking with his boots up on the barn, on the top.
Taking off from his shoulder-- I hear every move-- the rifle on the floor.
Taking off his aluminum canteen.
I hear the noise.
And I said, I'm in trouble.
If that SS finds out that a prisoner is laying underneath.
He's upstairs with his-- with his-- a gun, with his rifle.
I'm downstairs by the cows.
I'm a dead man.
I'm dead.
But he was hiding himself.
He asked the girl if she's got some clothes, civilian clothes.
I hear in German he's talking to her.
And she gave him some sort of a coat or something.
And he must have left his clothes right there, up.
Then the girl came in again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
The girl knocked me again on the shoulder quietly.
She said, SS man is up.
Don't say a word.
Don't talk.
Don't move.
We are all going to be shot.
She says, the Americans are a mile from my house.
It took maybe [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 20, I think, minutes.
Or 25.
I don't know the time.
I cannot tell you.
By the time-- to me it would have been like a year.
And she knock me on the shoulder, open the door.
I opened the door.
I walked out.
The Americans are here.
American soldiers with trucks, with Jeeps, with tanks, with ambulances trucks.
The Red Cross.
Black people.
I've never seen in my life a black man in my life, soldiers.
I looked at.
I says, where am I?
What is it?
What happened to my brother?
Right away, American soldier came to me.
He happens to be somewheres from Brooklyn.
And he spoke a little Jewish.
And he said, was bist Du?
[SPEAKING YIDDISH].
I said, yeah.
I talk Jewish.
I'm a Jew.
He says, you're a Jew?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
Can-- didn't talk too much Jewish.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH].
And right away, he gave me something to drink, like a soda or something to drink.
And he took right away a round little box and he peeled off, and he took out like a brown piece of cake, like a doughnut.
Eat.
And my hands were shaking.
And another soldier came, and he gave me chocolate.
And another chocolate said, give him better the beans.
It's warm still.
A can of white beans, all, you know, Army food.
And all with-- came with photographs, with cameras, and start taking my picture.
And I cried.
I says, what are you-- what-- and then a Polack came, a soldier, was talking Polish.
He said to me, [SPEAKING POLISH].
In Polish.
You understand Polish?
I said to him, I understand a little bit.
And then another one came who talk already better Yiddish.
Understand?
Jewish.
And I said, go fast right in that valley.
It's 10 minutes from you.
There's 100,000 maybe prisoners to be shot.
And my brother is there.
And they gave a run with the cars and with the Jeeps, all around that place there.
And before they ran and they ran away, and still a lot of soldiers and a black man was looking at me, and he gave me candy.
And I looked, I said, I never seen a black man, you know?
And I was-- so interesting for me to see these type people.
And they gave me so much food, and I had a big, you know, [GERMAN].
Like a big what the Germans wear, you know, on their shoulder.
A-- a rucksack.
You know?
I got a rucksack.
The girl gave me from the farm a rucksack.
She throw me out.
They saw she's giving me all that food, so she throw out a rucksack.
She gave it to me.
And they loaded me up with food.
And I had so much I couldn't eat no more.
And they took pictures and they-- they didn't know what to do, the American soldiers, to me.
And I grabbed one soldier, and I kissed him.
And then I forgot all about.
I said to the soldier, there's SS man hiding himself upstairs.
I was so confused.
I was so out of this world that my main enemy, my guard, the SS man is upstairs sitting there and hiding himself.
He thinks he's going to get away with murder.
So I said to one of the American soldiers who spoke already Jewish almost fluently.
I think he-- he spoke Jewish better than English.
He must have been maybe from the Delancey Street.
Got-- I mean, real good.
Oy.
I am so verklempt.
Verklempt.
And I still-- and I still got to talk, you know, a little Jewish to explain you.
You don't-- maybe don't understand.
But I am telling English.
The soldier, the American soldier, came up.
And he run with such a speed, and he took off his gun.
And he said, Americans.
Down.
And he said to me to tell him.
He called me too.
I should go with him.
Tell him to go down.
And I told him in German [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Go down.
The American soldier is here.
And the American soldier said to him-- he knew in English, that soldier, to say with the hands up.
And he came from the steps down like that with a big coat the girl found somewhere, a torn up coat.
It-- and underneath, he was almost naked because he threw away all the uniform there.
All wrapped up in that coat like that.
And he walked-- and he told him again.
Hands up.
And he was afraid, you know, the coat is going to fall off of him or something.
And that-- that German-- the American almost shot him because he start to move his hand.
And he was going like that.
And they took him right downstairs.
And while they took him downstairs, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] he said to me, that American soldier-- I wish I knew who it was.
I should have taken his name.
I would kiss-- I would hug him.
I wouldn't know what to do with that man.
He said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN], what's your name?
I said, Moishe.
He said, in America we call you maybe Morris or maybe Marty or maybe Moe.
[YIDDISH], he said in Jewish.
He says, Morris, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if you want to knock his face off, do it right now.
Beat him, that SS man.
I was a skeleton.
I weighed 60-- about 68 pounds.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Almost like 33 kilos.
That was my weight.
Kilogram almost. 68 pounds or 64 pound.
I got to look it up from [INAUDIBLE] when they weighed me, you know.
My-- my weight.
That's what I weighted.
About 33 kilos.
And I was about, you know, to give him that real punch in his face and-- and tell him apart.
But I gave him a little punch and little thing, I thought I almost broke my wrist here.
The man said, I know you couldn't beat him.
We are going to do it for you.
And he gave him a couple of kicks, you know, and pushes.
And he pushed him right, you know, in front of the trucks.
And he told him to sit down.
And he was searched again, you know, underneath the coat and everything.
And they took his rifle from upstairs.
Brought it down, all his things.
And in the building, in the corner of the house, they took the rifle, the American soldier, and they broke it, you know.
That was-- they did to all the rifles.
So I said to myself, the Americans, they must be the richest home in the world.
They take new rifles, good ones, and they all break them in-- in pieces and-- and making all big piles.
To me, it was, you know, like-- like a mystery.
But that was the law.
To break all the German rifles and stack them up.
And then they went right to that big valley.
They arrested all the Germans and the SS.
And they let out all the prisoners to go in that little town, Greiling and around Bad Toelz.
And when everybody start to walk around in Greiling, and my brothers-- my brother noticed me crawling in, but he didn't have a chance.
The other guards watched him, maybe.
Saw him.
He couldn't have a chance.
So he looked at that gutter, you know.
So he figured I must be right there near that-- So you found your brother?
--that sewer.
Near that big sewer line there.
And he walked right in in the house.
And he saw me.
And he said to me, what are you doing at that stables?
The Americans are here.
I says, I know, brother.
Here is all the foods the Americans gave me.
Sit down and eat!
And we sat down in the stable both, and we start eating.
And I was already too full, but my brother start eating.
And we ate so much in that stable.
And we were liberated in that stable by the American, God blessed Army.
I appreciate.
